# K-12: Codex Lesson Planning

<!-- vimeo: 1197917755 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

In this demo, I'll show how a teacher can use Codex as a lesson planning partner when the important context is spread across multiple documents. Here, I have a small folder with three files for my sixth-grade ratios unit. I have the Common Core State Standards for math. I have a scope and sequence calendar, which outlines the lessons throughout the unit, and today's lesson on unit rates that I need to plan for. And I then have notes about the needs and context of the class, including student profiles, accommodations, and supports for a proper lesson. I now turn to Codex to begin the lesson planning process. In the prompt, I tell Codex information about myself to help with the lesson planning. I share where the documents are, which is the Google Drive folder for sixth-grade ratios, and then ask to create a 50-minute lesson plan on day five. I give the components of the lesson, including materials, objective, and timing, and then I specify the output, a Word document as a table. I kick off the process and let Codex do its work. Codex has created a first draft of the lesson plan. Let's check it out. This output is a complete lesson plan I can review and adjust. I see the core components are in place. I have the objective, teacher moves throughout the lesson, timing, and I even have an exit ticket and closure. However, I know it's not fully reflective of what I know about this specific group of students and what they need in my teaching tomorrow. Let's revise this lesson plan with Codex. I ask Codex to revise this lesson plan for the following changes, adding additional support for multilingual learners, providing sentence frames to help with student discussions, planning for any potential misconceptions and what I can do to get ahead of that, and then any reteach plan for the next day for students who missed the exit ticket. Let's have Codex make these changes. This revision is important because it allows me to stay in control while Codex does the extra work to revise. I review this lesson, and I see there's a few different changes. There's additional sentence stems located throughout the lesson. The supports for multilingual learners are added, and at the end, there's that reteach plan for missed exit tickets that I asked Codex to put in. What I like about this workflow is Codex doesn't replace the teacher's decisions and planning. It helps turn existing materials into an editable draft faster, and then the teacher can refine it based on what students actually need.
